paper_lookup.title: Ultrafast and memory-efficient alignment of short DNA sequences to the human genome
paper_lookup.abstract: Bowtie is an ultrafast, memory-efficient alignment program for aligning short DNA sequence reads to large genomes. For the human genome, Burrows-Wheeler indexing allows Bowtie to align more than 25 million reads per CPU hour with a memory footprint of approximately 1.3 gigabytes. Bowtie extends previous Burrows-Wheeler techniques with a novel quality-aware backtracking algorithm that permits mismatches. Multiple processor cores can be used simultaneously to achieve even greater alignment speeds. Bowtie is open source http://bowtie.cbcb.umd.edu.

linked_paper_summary: The referenced 2009 paper describes Bowtie, an open-source, ultrafast and memory-efficient aligner for mapping short DNA sequence reads to large genomes using a Burrows–Wheeler index and a quality‑aware backtracking algorithm. Bowtie achieves very high throughput with a small memory footprint and supports multithreading; the original Bowtie (v1) is optimized for ungapped, mismatch-tolerant alignments of short sequences. In the cited study, Bowtie is used to align PCR primers to genomes to infer off-target amplification: primer binding sites and properly oriented primer pairs within a specified distance (≤2,200 bp) are identified to predict potential amplicons.

paper_lookup.title: GTDB: an ongoing census of bacterial and archaeal diversity through a phylogenetically consistent, rank normalized and complete genome-based taxonomy
paper_lookup.abstract: Abstract The Genome Taxonomy Database (GTDB; https://gtdb.ecogenomic.org) provides a phylogenetically consistent and rank normalized genome-based taxonomy for prokaryotic genomes sourced from the NCBI Assembly database. GTDB R06-RS202 spans 254 090 bacterial and 4316 archaeal genomes, a 270% increase since the introduction of the GTDB in November, 2017. These genomes are organized into 45 555 bacterial and 2339 archaeal species clusters which is a 200% increase since the integration of species clusters into the GTDB in June, 2019. Here, we explore prokaryotic diversity from the perspective of the GTDB and highlight the importance of metagenome-assembled genomes in expanding available genomic representation. We also discuss improvements to the GTDB website which allow tracking of taxonomic changes, easy assessment of genome assembly quality, and identification of genomes assembled from type material or used as species representatives. Methodological updates and policy changes made since the inception of the GTDB are then described along with the procedure used to update species clusters in the GTDB. We conclude with a discussion on the use of average nucleotide identities as a pragmatic approach for delineating prokaryotic species.

linked_paper_summary: This paper describes the Genome Taxonomy Database (GTDB), a curated, genome-based, phylogenetically consistent and rank-normalized taxonomy for bacteria and archaea built from genomes in NCBI Assembly (now spanning ~254k bacterial and ~4.3k archaeal genomes organized into >45k bacterial and >2.3k archaeal species clusters). It documents methodological and policy updates, the incorporation and importance of metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs), improvements to the GTDB website (tracking taxonomic changes, assembly quality, and type-material representatives), and the practical use of average nucleotide identity (ANI) for species delineation. The GTDB serves as an authoritative reference taxonomy widely used to assign taxonomic labels and lineages, support comparative genomics, and underpin bioinformatic tools and panels (e.g., for lineage assignment and interpretation in microbial resistance/typing workflows).

paper_lookup.title: Rapid antibiotic-resistance predictions from genome sequence data for Staphylococcus aureus and Mycobacterium tuberculosis
paper_lookup.abstract: The rise of antibiotic-resistant bacteria has led to an urgent need for rapid detection of drug resistance in clinical samples, and improvements in global surveillance. Here we show how de Bruijn graph representation of bacterial diversity can be used to identify species and resistance profiles of clinical isolates. We implement this method for Staphylococcus aureus and Mycobacterium tuberculosis in a software package (‘Mykrobe predictor’) that takes raw sequence data as input, and generates a clinician-friendly report within 3 minutes on a laptop. For S. aureus, the error rates of our method are comparable to gold-standard phenotypic methods, with sensitivity/specificity of 99.1%/99.6% across 12 antibiotics (using an independent validation set, n=470). For M. tuberculosis, our method predicts resistance with sensitivity/specificity of 82.6%/98.5% (independent validation set, n=1,609); sensitivity is lower here, probably because of limited understanding of the underlying genetic mechanisms. We give evidence that minor alleles improve detection of extremely drug-resistant strains, and demonstrate feasibility of the use of emerging single-molecule nanopore sequencing techniques for these purposes. The clinical application of new sequencing techniques is expected to accelerate pathogen identification. Here, Bradley et al. present a clinician-friendly software package that uses sequencing data for quick and accurate prediction of antibiotic resistance profiles for S. aureus and M. tuberculosis.

linked_paper_summary: This paper describes Mykrobe Predictor, a clinician-focused software tool that uses a de Bruijn graph representation of bacterial sequence diversity to identify species and predict antibiotic-resistance profiles directly from raw genome-sequencing data in minutes. The authors validate the approach on Staphylococcus aureus (sensitivity/specificity 99.1%/99.6% across 12 antibiotics, n=470) and Mycobacterium tuberculosis (82.6%/98.5%, n=1,609), noting lower M. tuberculosis sensitivity likely due to incomplete knowledge of resistance mechanisms. They also show that detecting minor alleles can help identify highly resistant strains and demonstrate feasibility with nanopore single-molecule sequencing. The paper’s role is to provide a fast, validated computational method and software implementation that advances practical clinical diagnostics and global surveillance of antibiotic resistance, while highlighting current limitations and opportunities for improvement as resistance-genotype knowledge and sequencing technologies evolve.
